name: Physical
description: Moves raw bits across a physical medium — voltages, light pulses, or radio waves. Defines cabling, connectors, pin layouts, modulation schemes, and signal timing. Knows nothing about meaning; its only job is to put a 1 or a 0 on the wire and recover it on the other end.
examples: RJ45 connectors, BNC, ST/LC fibre connectors
DSL, ISDN, SONET/SDH, USB, Bluetooth radio

name: Data Link
description: Frames raw bits into structured units, addresses them on the local segment, and detects (sometimes corrects) transmission errors. Split into LLC (logical link control) and MAC (media access control) sublayers. Operates only within a single broadcast domain — it has no concept of routing between networks.
examples: ARP — resolves IP addresses to MAC addresses
VLANs (802.1Q), STP, PPP, HDLC, Frame Relay

name: Network
description: Delivers packets between hosts across multiple networks using logical addresses. Decides the path each packet takes (routing), fragments oversized packets, and handles inter-network reachability. Best-effort by design — no guarantees of delivery, ordering, or duplicate prevention; that's left to higher layers.
examples: IPv4 and IPv6 addressing and packet headers
Routing protocols — OSPF, BGP, RIP, EIGRP
Routers, layer-3 switches, firewalls operating on IP

name: Transport
description: End-to-end delivery between processes on hosts, identified by port numbers. Adds reliability features: connection setup, segmentation and reassembly, flow control, congestion control, retransmission, and ordered delivery — or deliberately omits them for speed. The boundary between 'the network' and 'the application'.
examples: SCTP — multi-streaming, used in telecom signalling
Port numbers (0–65535), TCP three-way handshake, sequence/ack numbers

name: Session
description: Establishes, manages, and tears down conversations between applications. Handles dialogue control (who speaks when), checkpointing for recovery, and session resumption after a network blip. Often thin or absent in the TCP/IP stack — many of its responsibilities have been absorbed into the application or transport layers.
examples: SQL session establishment, SMB session setup

name: Presentation
description: Translates between the application's data formats and a canonical wire representation. Handles character encoding, serialisation, compression, and encryption — making sure the receiving application can interpret what the sender produced even if their internal formats differ.
examples: TLS / SSL — encryption and integrity for application data
Character encodings — ASCII, UTF-8, UTF-16, EBCDIC
Media encodings — JPEG, PNG, GIF, MPEG, MP3
Serialisation formats — XML, JSON, ASN.1, Protocol Buffers
MIME types, gzip / deflate / Brotli compression

name: Application
description: The layer the user (or user's software) directly interacts with. Defines the protocols and message formats that applications speak — HTTP requests, DNS queries, mail envelopes, file transfers. Where most real-world security incidents land: misconfigurations, broken auth, injection, business-logic abuse all live here.
examples: SSH, FTP, SFTP, SCP — remote access and file transfer
